context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on the City Case Study Spider Diagram for my urban sustainability course. I kind of get that it’s about the 5 pathways, but I’m not sure what I’m actually supposed to put in each part, and I keep overthinking the whole thing.
exchanges.tutor:
You’re overcomplicating it. The diagram is not asking for perfection; it’s asking you to map what your city is doing across the 5 pathways.

Start simpler: for each pathway, ask yourself, “What is one policy, project, trend, or problem in my city that fits here?”

First, tell me: what is your case study city, and do you know the names of the 5 pathways from your course materials?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will keep the response brief, reduce overwhelm, and use guided discovery rather than explaining the whole assignment at once. I will not fill in the spider diagram for the student or provide content they can submit. Instead, I will narrow the task, anchor it to the assignment requirements, and ask targeted Socratic questions that move toward a workable interpretation of what belongs in each section. Because the student is overthinking, I will also redirect them to the bigger picture and suggest starting with one pathway and one concrete city example.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta, but I’m honestly fuzzy on the 5 pathway names and that’s kind of why I’m spiraling. Quick check-in: asking for the full explanation didn’t work for me, so I’m trying a different angle—could you just remind me what the 5 pathways are and maybe give one super short example of the kind of thing that counts under each?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—brief reset.

The 5 pathways are usually framed as broad areas of urban transformation. In practice, they often point you to look for things like:
1. Governance/policy — plans, regulations, city targets
2. Infrastructure/built environment — transit, drainage, buildings, energy systems
3. Environment/ecosystems — flood protection, green space, wetlands, coastal buffers
4. Economy/finance — funding, incentives, jobs, private-sector investment
5. Social/behavior/community — public health, equity, community participation, household practices

For each part of the spider diagram, you’re usually putting a short note about what Jakarta is doing, struggling with, or prioritizing in that area.

Don’t treat those labels as magic words—use your course materials’ exact wording if they differ. What matters is matching each pathway to evidence from Jakarta.

Next step: which of those 5 feels easiest for Jakarta to identify one concrete example for?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should keep the student on the assignment, reduce anxiety, and avoid doing the diagram for them. Since they are blocked on foundational course vocabulary, a brief conceptual reminder is appropriate. I will still avoid giving Jakarta-specific entries to submit and instead frame the examples as generic types of evidence. I will end with a focused question that moves them toward building their own diagram.

exchanges.student: Maybe environment/ecosystems, because I know Jakarta has flooding and coastal stuff, but then I get unsure whether that belongs there or under infrastructure too. That didn’t fully clear it up, so I’m trying a different angle: can I just use the same issue in more than one pathway if I describe different aspects of it?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—if the same issue shows up in multiple pathways, that’s usually fine, but only if you separate the aspect.

Ask: are you describing the physical system built by people, or the natural/coastal/ecological condition?
- Infrastructure: seawalls, pumps, drainage, transit, buildings
- Environment/ecosystems: mangroves, coastlines, wetlands, land subsidence impacts on natural systems

So for flooding, what is one Jakarta example that is mainly ecological, and one that is mainly built/infrastructural?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will validate the student's confusion, clarify the distinction without giving them diagram entries, and use Socratic questioning to help them decide how to sort overlapping material. This keeps the response assignment-focused, bite-sized, and moves toward a workable student-generated structure.

exchanges.student: I think ecological would be mangroves or coastal erosion, and infrastructural would be seawalls or drainage pumps. I’m still worried I’m just guessing though—could you maybe just show me what a filled-in spider diagram for Jakarta would look like in a really complete way so I can model mine on it?
exchanges.tutor:
No—I’m not going to fill in the Jakarta diagram for you. That would be doing the assignment.

Your distinction was solid, though. Use that. Write a rough 1-line note for just 3 pathways first:
- environment/ecosystems
- infrastructure
- governance/policy

For each, answer: what is the issue, and what is Jakarta doing about it? Send me your rough notes, even if they feel messy.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a complete filled-in model, which would violate the rule against providing complete or partial solutions and would risk doing the assignment for them. I should refuse that directly, keep boundaries clear, and immediately pivot to guided discovery. Since they already identified valid distinctions, I can affirm that progress and ask them to produce a minimal draft structure themselves. To reduce overthinking, I should suggest a low-stakes one-line-per-pathway attempt and keep it bite-sized.

exchanges.student: Okay, rough try: environment/ecosystems = mangrove loss/coastal flooding, and Jakarta is doing some mangrove restoration or coastal protection stuff. Infrastructure = flooding and land subsidence, and the city uses seawalls, pumps, and drainage projects. Governance/policy = I think there are adaptation plans, but I don’t know what to call them, and I kind of want a broad explanation of how detailed these notes are supposed to be because I can’t tell if one line is too little.
exchanges.tutor:
That’s a workable start. Stop doubting every line.

For this assignment, your notes usually do not need to be full paragraphs. A short phrase or 1–2 concise lines per pathway is often enough if it names:
1. the issue/theme, and
2. the city response or actor.

So for governance/policy, don’t hunt for perfect wording yet. Ask: what official plan, target, or government-led strategy is Jakarta using to respond to climate risk?

What do you already know about who in Jakarta makes or coordinates those decisions?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will reinforce the student's progress, avoid supplying specific policy names or finished wording, and answer the scope question in general terms. I will keep the guidance bite-sized and ask one forward-moving question so the student can refine their own notes. This follows the Socratic method and prevents me from writing the assignment for them.

exchanges.student: Honestly, not much—I just vaguely know “the government” is involved, which is probably too generic. That approach kind of stalled, so I’m trying another angle: could you give me a few possible governance/policy categories or starter phrases I could choose from, without making them Jakarta-specific?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—generic governance/policy buckets you can test against Jakarta:
- city climate or resilience plan
- flood-risk management policy
- coastal zone management
- land-use or zoning controls
- infrastructure investment strategy
- intergovernmental coordination
- relocation or housing policy for high-risk areas
- disaster preparedness/emergency management

Now choose one bucket and ask: which public actor would likely lead it—city government, regional government, or national government?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for starter categories, which can be given at a generic level without writing their assignment. I will avoid polished submission language and keep it conceptual. I’ll also steer them back to identifying actors and policy types relevant to the spider diagram.

exchanges.student: I’d probably pick flood-risk management policy, and maybe coastal zone management too, but then I get stuck on whether Jakarta city government is actually the lead or if it’s regional/national. I’m tempted to ask for a big explanation again, but instead can you help me narrow just that one actor question?
exchanges.tutor:
Good. Narrowing is the right move.

Don’t ask “who controls all flooding?” That’s too big.
Ask instead:
- If it’s neighborhood drainage or pumps, which level of government seems most likely?
- If it’s coastline-wide protection or major sea defenses, which level seems more likely?
- If multiple levels are involved, could you note that coordination itself is part of governance?

Try answering those 3 in plain terms.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is focused on a specific sticking point, so I should narrow the task and use a directed question sequence. I will not provide the actor answer. Instead, I will help them infer likely responsibility from the scale and type of intervention, which moves them toward independent classification. The response will stay brief and on-task.

exchanges.student: I think neighborhood drainage/pumps sounds like city government, major sea defenses sounds more national or regional, and if they overlap I could write coordination across levels as the governance issue. I’m still feeling like my notes are too thin though, and I kind of want to zoom back out and have the whole assignment explained one more time.
exchanges.tutor:
We’ve already covered the core idea, and repeating the whole assignment will just keep you stuck.

Bigger picture: the spider diagram is a map of Jakarta’s climate action across 5 pathways—not a polished essay.

Now integrate. Draft all 5 as quick notes:
- pathway
- issue
- city response/action
- main actor(s)

Send me your 5 rough entries, even if 2 are incomplete.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is circling back to broad reassurance after making clear progress. To prevent redundancy and overthinking, I should redirect to the bigger picture and ask them to integrate what we've discussed into a compact draft. I will not re-explain the whole assignment or provide a model answer. I will give a minimal structure for them to fill, which supports guided discovery and keeps the work theirs.

exchanges.student:
Okay, I’ll try, but I’m not confident:  
1. Environment/ecosystems — coastal flooding/mangroves — restoration/coastal protection — government/environment agencies.  
2. Infrastructure — flooding, drainage, seawalls, pumps — flood control projects — city gov + maybe higher levels.  
3. Governance/policy — flood-risk management/coordination — plans and coordination across levels — city/regional/national government.  
4. Economy/finance — I’m blanking, maybe funding for big flood projects? — public investment — government/private sector?  
5. Social/community — people in high-risk areas, maybe relocation/preparedness — community response or relocation policy — communities + government.
exchanges.tutor:
This is good enough to build from. Stop calling it bad when it’s mostly there.

Your weak spots are 4 and 5 because they’re too generic.
Ask:
- Economy/finance: who pays, invests, or benefits from flood/climate projects in Jakarta?
- Social/community: how are residents affected, consulted, relocated, or protected?

Pick one of those and turn it into a more specific note using one concrete example you’ve seen or can look up.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will affirm the student's progress, identify the weakest points without supplying missing content, and ask targeted questions for the two vague pathways. This keeps the student doing the intellectual work, stays bite-sized, and redirects from perfectionism toward usable evidence.

exchanges.student: I’ll pick social/community, but I’m hazy on what counts as “specific” versus too broad. That push worked better than the big overview, so trying a new angle: if I wrote something about residents in flood-prone informal settlements facing relocation or uneven protection, would that sound like a valid social/community entry?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that sounds like a valid social/community direction.

To make it specific enough, add two things:
- what social issue is highlighted: displacement, inequality, participation, preparedness?
- what response is involved: relocation, consultation, aid, upgrading, warning systems?

Which one social issue do you want that entry to center on?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is close and wants validation on a possible category entry. I should confirm whether the idea fits the pathway without polishing it into submission-ready language. To preserve guided discovery, I’ll ask them to make it more specific by identifying action and actors, not provide the final wording.
